CURRENT AFFAIRS.

It was only a few days ago that Boyle of the Testi-
monials, in the County Roscommnion, taxed itself for a
gratuitous presentation to a clerk who was leaving the
town on promotion to the bigoted Bank of Ireland in
Clonakilty. Now the hat is going round again for a
“landlord, one Edward Stafford King-Harman, who, if
God spares him, will be 21 on April 13th next. One of
the fundamental deficiencies of the Anglicised Irish race
is the lack of a sense of humour. Bobbies and bank
clerks are the great marks for testimonials, and that

" is bad enough. But surely the landlords have had
enough out of the country and surely the price of their
land has been sufficiently inflated by recent legislation
that landlords at least might be allowed to come of

- age ab their own eXpense and not be an additional tax
- on poor people. Lloyd George’s Budget was to work

we do not know, what disasters in Ireland, yet in Boyle

there is money for a bank clerk’s testimonial a few
days ago, and now the hat is going roun(} for a
boy of the King-Harman’s. There was a public meet-
ing held in Boyle last week to promote this ** nation-
making”’ industry of testimonials. A-Major Murphy
was in the chair. We are quite willing to thoroughly
agree with him when he said, *‘ I wish to say, as re-
gards this town and district, that no people I have
ever met in all my wanderings are kinder in this sort
of way than the people of Boyle.”” We suppose Boyle
of the testimonials has smacked its lips at that: if it
had any sense of humour it would smack the ridicu-
lous custom of absurd testimonial giving. We see that
W. J. Robinson, locally known as the British Lion, was
to the fore in this testimonial as he was in that to the
clerk a few days ago; and so also was Mr. M. Candon.
These pair must get money very easily they are so
quick to part with it for testimonials. The name of
Myr. James Quinn, of Drum, was suggested for the
committee, but Mr. Quinn apparently has-a sense of
proportion and a sense of the ridiculous, so he said :—
*“I object. I don’t see what is the occasion for re-
joicing in fhe coming of age of young Mu.  Stafford
King-Harman. Dr. Stafford is a fortunate man, and
he has everything that the eye can see or the heart of
man desive——.""  The local report adds:—'‘ Here
Mr. Quinn was interrupted, and although he essayed
to speak several times, he could not proceed.’’

The limit to the subseriptions for the bank clerk a
few days ago was £1, but the limit for the landlord
was fixed at £1 1s.—a shilling higher. The British
Lion, Robinson, only gave 10s. and a bank clerk named
Manuning also gave 10s. Is it not hard for a bank
clerk—probably trying to do the shoneen on 25s. a week
—to have to give 10s. to a landlord : but in due course
we suppose he expects to get a testimonial, and the
British Lion of Boyle will have to plank out again.

It is a bad wind that blows nobody good. We read
in a local paper:—'‘ As a result of the coal strike,
another porter famine is anticipated in Granard. The
railway company, it appears, are only carrying provi-
sions and perishable goods. As porter does not come
under these heads, it will remain behind, so that when
the present supply is exhausted, compulsory pledges
will be the order of the day.”

If Irish papers took to the practice of expressing
disgust and indignation at English crimes what a job
they would have before them. There was a man
named Seddon sentenced to death for poisoning some
woman in London. Needless to say we did not read the
case, we have something better to’occupyour minds
than the' details of English poisoners.
tion was attracted by the words “ Masonic sign’’ in
one of the reports of the final proceedings. The report
in ““ Buff ”’ says:—

As he made his appeal to the Great Architect,
he raised his hand, pointed aloft, and gave the
Masonic sign. Every Freemason in court saw
this, and naturally it did not escape Mr. Justice
Bucknill, who is provincial grand master of Sur-
rey It evidently intensified his emotion.  He
could scarcely speak. He had to choke back the
tears to cough oub the ends of his sentences. He
could only just be heard. ?

So this English criminal was a n-sinber of fhe perni-
cious secret society, the Free and Accepted Masons,
and when the condemned murderer gave ‘‘the sign,”

But our atben== —

that fact, a;sco:'ding to ““ Buff’s *’ report, *‘ evidently
intensified his (the judge’s) emotion.’’

But the ‘‘sign” apparently did not work in this case,
for ‘the Freemason murderer was sentenced to death.
But there was another little bit of Freemason by-play
before the end. We take the following from ‘‘Buff’ :—

The judge paid no attention, but continued his
kindly address. ‘ Try in the short time left you
to make peace with your Maker,” he urged.

“I am already at peace,” Seddon flung back
to him. ‘ We both belong to the same brother-
hood.””

The judge faltered, ‘“and though that can

3 have no influence with me, yet I pray you again to
make your peace with the Great Architect of the
Universe to Whom you are referred.”

So the judge and the murderer are both “‘brothers’!
in the same brotherhood with Brother Goulding and
all the other members of this secret society ? We do
not know to what extent if any the laws of the secret
Freemasons brush aside and trample on the laws of
the land. But the emotion of Brother Judge, accord-
ing to the report, was intensified when Brother
Murderer revealed that he too was a Freemason. Sup-
posing it had been a case of Brother Seddon in a civil
case against some respectable man who would not
touch the secret society with a forty-foot pole, and if
Brother Seddon gave the ‘‘sign’’ to Brother Judge
what would Brother Judge be supposed to do as a true
member of the secret society of Freemasons?  This
pernicious secret society should be abolished by law.
Being secret, the public do not know what underground
things are being carried on: but in Ireland the verdict
of public opinion has it that its members are tricking
and wirepulling and boycotting in official jobs, promo-
tions and in business, and it is a pest in the country.

An Trish Treland friend of ours—a well known medi-
cal man—recently received a circular about an English
preparatory school in the County of Dublin.  The
school it appears was founded in 1904 “ to provide an
English preparatory school education in Ireland.”” The
circular also states, ‘‘ Since then it has had marked
success in preparing boys for the English public schools
and Osborne.”” The gentleman who carries on this
enterprise should circularise the Carsonites and that
lot: it is only waste of stamps to be sending circulars
to Irish Irelanders.

The Methodists were on tap against Ireland in
Belfast last week. A large number of Methodists
make a good living out of Trish custom, yet the Metho-
dists, as such, congregate in Belfast to spew out anti-
Trish and anti-Catholic venom. To emphasise the sec-
tarian nature of the bigotry, the meeting at the Ulster
Hall was opened with prayer by the Rev. Wesley
Guard. Sir George Chubb, whoever he is, wrote in-
dignantly against ‘‘ the monstrous and unfair propo-
sals to pub the future government of my co-religionists
of Ireland under the yoke of the Pope of Rome.’”

__—— The resolution set out that ““ This meeting of Metho-
3 dists from every part of Ireland, and representing the
vast majority of Irish Methodists, affirms,”” ete. It
would take too much space to set out the long-winded
resolutions in full. We see a reference to the wrong
that Home Rule would do, ‘‘ especially upon the loyal,

portion of the community’”
—we suppose these are the Methodists u.uq other non-
Catholics! The way some of these bounding and im-
pertinent bigots talk, you v:r‘ogld think there was not a
prosperous Or independent idolater ’’ in t}:e country.
It appears Home Rule, amongst othgr things, would
imperil the ‘‘ best interests of education, _bemperance,
and Sabbath observance.”’ Is not L'l'm_; impertinence
gone crazy ? ‘Of course “‘ Ne Temere” is hawked out,
But these liberty-loving Methodists give us no” hope
that under Home Rule or under the_Union they will
do anything for us poor pcrsecpbed Pa.p]s(;'s, who are
not allowed to eat meat on Ifrldn»ys, a thing bhat., as
members of the British hEmpire we have a legnl right
to do! Why don’t the Methodists, Presbyter{ans and
the rest start an agitation against free-born Britons, as
they would style us, being forced to refram'from meat
by the * Italian mission ’|  Perhaps their business
interests are greater in fish than in meat!

law-abiding and prosperous

The note in the speeches, as 1'epqrted in the ““ Trish
Times’ is anti-Catholic and insultingly so-—the pro-
position is that they could not trust themselves to get
justice if Ireland had Home Rule in view of the fact
that the preponderating majority in Treland are Catho-
lics. The attitude is mosb insulting and impudent.
The chairman, whoever he was, remarked :—

They cordially welcomed their English friends,
and hoped when they returned they would make
it known far and wide that they were deeply in
earnest in this matter. It was a great and serious
crisis in their history, and their chief hope was
that their Nonconformist brethren would come to
the rescue, so that as industrious and loyal British -
subjects they should get peace to follow their
various callings and duties, and live in harmony
with their neighbours, and enjoy the blessings of
civil and religious liberty under the protection of
the British flag, as their forefathers did. ¥

We suppose they will all be murdered in their beds
when we get Home Rule!

The anti-Catholic drum appears to be the chief in-
strument of the anti-Home Rule band. The Rev. G.
R. Wedgewood, in the course of his remarks, said :—

Those who said it was not a religious question
had closed their eyes not only to the ecclesiastical
history of the country, but to the arrogant claims
which the Church of Rome had been pressing in
recent years. With the Roman Catholic Hier-
archy it was all take and no give. They could not
lend themselves to increasing this political-eccle-
siastical power. That illogical lover of political
justice, Dr. Horton, said that Home Rule meant
Rome Rule, and a bad time for us. Many of the
same kith said the same. And why not? They
are in the majority. But they were further told
that such had been the progress of truth and tole-
ration that now Roman Catholicism was not what
it was. They might think they were playing with
a cooing dove; but ere long they might find that
the imaginary dove was really a cunning fox wait-
ing his opportunity to strangle and run away with
the goose that laid the golden eggs. ILet them
dream on: they would get a rude awakening some
day. Who persecuted the evangelists in Co. Wex-
ford? Who persecuted the evangelists at Swords

in Co. Dublin? - Who persecuted the Ballintoy

MILLINERY. =

2

post-mistress, and prevented her earning her liv-
ing? Who led on a mob with sticks and stones
at Sligo and_Clifden to attack William Harpur
and his-assistants ? They all knew that when the
police prosecuted they could not get a conviction,
and he had not been able to go there since. Why,
even that paragon of consistency who presided at
the Agricultural Board had told them that when
Home Rule came it would mean 20 years of hell.
But we were all trying to give that place a wide
berth. Popery had never kept faith with heretics,
and it never would until ‘ the priests themselves
believe and put salvation on.”” TFor this reason
they could not trust it. There were no opportu-
nists in the world like the Jesuits. And, there-
fore, everybody should be made to feel that this
crisis threatened them with an awful religious
calamity. And so to-day, as of old, they stood
for a free Bible, a free conscience, a free Press,
personal, civil, and religious liberty. \

This is a raimeis harangue to be smiled at: you
cannot really argue with stuff of that kind, but it is well
to place it on record in our columns. But by the way
of sporting comment, might we ask a few questions:—
Who cruelly libelled Canon MecInerney ? \Who libelled
the Bishop of Cloyne and six Queenstown priests and
had to pay for doing so? Who staffs the Provincial
Bank, the Great Sourface and the Great Northern
Railway ? Who assaulted Lord Pirrie?  Who out-
raged freedom of speech in Belfast? Who attacked
the Mater Hospital in Belfast? Whose most popular
prayer is ‘‘ To hell with the Pope?” Who slanders
the Jesuits? We could go on for days asking such
questions, but we have asked enough. We may add
one more. Who are the Methodists chief customers
in the South and West ?

Mr. J. B. McCutcheon was careful to point out that
this Methodist meeting was not a political one.  If
that be so, what else was it but an anti-Catholic meet-
ing.  Mr. McCutcheon, in the course of his speech,
said :—

This was no political gathering. ~ Those who
were assembled represented all shades of political
opinion, some who would describe themselves as
Conservatives, many more, he had no doubt, who
in the days prior to 1886 belonged to the Liberal
Party, and would still belong to it could it only
free itself from the incubus of Home Rule. It
was impossible that there ever could have been
a movement more free from the initiative or stage-
management of politicians than the movement
which had brought them there that day. It had

been from first to last absolutely spontaneous, and

that meeting was the outward expression 'aud em-
bodiment of irresistible inward forces which were
bound to break forth and find a vent for them-
selves in this or some other form. They could nob
but regard as groundless the hopes of those who
_told them that Home Rule would lead to the
emancipation of Ireland from ecclesiastical con-
trol, nor did they look upon it as a thing to be
desired that such emancipation should be arrived
at, if at all, and after long years during which
irreparable evil might be wrought by the substitu-
tion of infidelity and ireligion for a definite reli-
gious system, as has been the case elsewhere, The
controlling force in Trish politics under Home Rule

would be a foreign Hierarchy, which pursued with
rare skill and persistency and unchanged and un-
changeable policy, which asserted by its arbitrary
decree its superiority to the law of the land in
@ matters touching the family, the home, and the

administration of the law itself, from which no
secrets were or could be hid, and which enforced

its decrees by the dread sentence of excommuni-

cation and terrors of the world 'to come. A Go-

vernment under such influences would not be one

to which they could look with any confidence to

maintain that religious equality for which they,

as Methodists, have always stood.

Rev. T. W. Davidson remarked :—

As a Protestant, and in the interests of fair play
and Protestantism, he protested with all his
might against Home Rule. Under a Dublin Par-
liament, Ireland would soon- become the most
Papal State in Europe. Knowing the real senti-
ments of the men behind the scenes—who had
expressed them frequently—was it any wonder
that they, Irish Methodists, as staunch Evange-
lical Christians, should resist such proposals to the
uttermost ?

A Brummagem*English M.P., named Parkes, would
not let go of politics in his speech, so he said :—* They
had present a combination of the political element with
the religious, which to his mind was the highest form
of patriotism.”’

What would Mr. Parkes think of our cry in Ireland
‘‘ Faith and Fatherland "’ ? That would not be to him
‘‘ the highest form of patriotism ’ we fear: his view
could only hold for the combination of ““ To hell with
the Pope and up for England’s Faithful Garrison in
Ireland.”” We would not think of arguing with many
offlhese spouters. For instance, this M.P., Mr. Parkes,
said :—

If he, as an English Nonconformist, was in any
doubt upon the question of Home Rule, he would
consider it his duty to consult an Irish Noncon-
formist, and ask him how it would affect him in
his liberty, his religion, and his social life, and if
he found it would injure him in these respects,
he would say that the Irishman’s opinion was
founded on better knowledge than he possessed,
and that he would follow him in this matter.

That means that as a non-Irish Nonconformist he would
place the interests of his sect foremost and intermeddle
with the Irish nation!

“In the course of evidence given recently in England
a witness connected with an English weekly journal,
the page of which is about the same size as a page of
the LEADER, was asked what the price of a full page
advertisement was at a certain period, and his reply
was, ‘‘ It varied from £60 or £70 to £120.”” What a
sidelight this throws on our Dark Brothers who won’t
a;ivel;tise at alll 'What do our huxter-publishers think
of it ?

We are glad to see that the P.M.G. has at last pro-.

mised that he will now look closely into the questions

we have dealt with concerning the Dublin Stores

Branch. We: hope that he will make a serious effort
to get at the real facts by independent inquiry and not
depend on exparte statements of London officials. Let
him seek the facts from the Dublin staff, who can tell

him where the shoe pinches. The plea that the ap-
pointment of the Birmingham staff officer to exercise.
** immediate superintendence’’ over the Dublin bmpch
merely represents the transfer of a headquarters officer
from London to Birmingham, and does not invol#e
the subordination of the lrish Stores Branch bas been
already exposed. Inquiries are promised nOW. But
we stick to the contention that the change that has
taken place has been the transfer of control from the
local officials in Dublin to the officials at Birmingham,
and that there has been no delegation of nu!;honty
from London to Birmingham so far as Dublin is con-
cerned. If the P.M.G.’s inquiry does not establish
these as facts let him publish the evidence on which
he contradicts our statements.

We say that the rccent changes have injuriously
affected the status of the Dublin office. Before these
changes took place the Birmingham Depol was con-
trolled from the Mount Pleasant Depot, London,
and did not even enjoy the same authority and status
as Dublin. The status of Birmingham has now been
improved and that of Dublin has been depressed. The
two main London depots, at Studd Street and Mount
Pleasant, are under entirely separate control. If such
separate control exists in London, where the depots
are only a few miles apart, where is the necessity for
placing the same officer in charge of such widely
separated branches as those of Birmingham and
Dublin ?

The expression Headquarters Officer as applied to
the official at Birmingham is absurd, for obv ¢ he
is no more a Headquarters official than the o s ab
Dublin. It is this transfer of authority, or ‘* imme-
diate superintendence,”’ as the Postmaster-General
prefers to call it, from the Irish metropolis to an En-
lish provincial city that we protest.against, and our
protest has been publicly endorsed by the chief muni-
cipal and commercial bodies of the city of Dublin, and
we learn with pleasure that other public bodies are
preparing to join in this protest. How can ‘‘imme-
diate superintendence’’ of the Dublin Depot be exer-
cised from Birmingham unless by the means con-
demned in advance by the third secretary to the Post
Office in hig recent address to the district telephone
managers of the country. Does Mr. Samuels really
think that he can afford to ignore the growing public
indignation concerning this matter, and retain his office
as a member of a Government pledged to govern Ire-
land in accordance with Irish ideas ?

It is indeed startling that with Home Rule on the
horizon a Home Rule Minister should be concerned in
placing a Dublin branch of the public service under—
Birmingham.  We are still awaiting Mr. Samuels’
reply-to the trenchant memorial addressed to him by
the staff on the proposed transfer of men to fill up the
vacancies in the local branch caused by the squeezing
out of the local staff that were transferred at the time
of the amalgamation with London. 5

And what about the Telephone Service?  What
about the establishment in Dublin.of a factory for the
repair of telephone apparatus used in Ireland ? Tf the
telephone service expands rapidly in Ireland such a
factory, which would employ a large number of hands
at the outset, would rapidly expand, and by providing
a reserve of skilled labour, might lay the foundation

for a big Government or private factory for the manu- *

facture of all the telephones required in the country.
The Industrial Development Association should look
after this.

. Archdeacon Flavin at the recent meeting of the Clon-

wardly consider it necessary at this period of our exi

with a capital subsecribed for all over Ireland by scme

~—Yours, faithfully, W. A. R. Cruisg, General Manager
and Secretary. ‘16th March, 1912. The Venerable
Archdeacon Flavin, D.D., P.P., Clonmel.—Very Rev.
and Dear Sir,—We have read with great interest your
vemarks at the Clonmel Asylum Committee on the
‘“ persecution” you have endured for the past three
years from the representative of this company in his
endeavours {o secure, your fire insurance business.
We quite recognise that such zeal is not common in
the prosecution of any business in Ireland, and are
therefore not surprised that you should speak of it as
‘persecuting and pestering you,”” but may we take the
liberty of suggesting that it might have been worth
your while to inquire who founded this company, and
what sort of a company it was, seeing that it was an
Irish company, and no doubt (like yourself) had special
interests in Ireland, mutual interests (yours and ours
combined), interests which did not extend beyond
the four provinces, and if, as the result of your investi-
gation, you had found that this company was quite as
certain -to fulfil its obligations as ** the biggest: com-
panies in the world,” may we further take the liberty
of pointing out to you that you would be studying the
interest of the asylum quite as much as our interest in
giving us your business. Seeing that the support of
Irish lunatic asylums has to be paid out of Irish re-
sources, and that even the millions of money to which

CARRICK-ON-SUIR No. 2 OLD AGE PENSION
SUB-COMMITTEE.

Dear Sir,—There are several mis-statements in your
article regarding this body published in your issue of
the 9th inst., .and which I desire to correct.  The
Treasury regulations 7¢ increased rate of pensions
are:—‘ If any pensioner desires to raise any question
as to the weekly rate of pension to which he is en-
titled he may send a statement of the question to-
gether with a summary of any evidence in support of
his allegations to the Pension Officer of the district in
which the pension is payable, and the Pension Officer
shall investigate and report to the Sub-Committee on
the question, in the same manner mutatis mutandis
as he is by the regulations required to do in the case
of a claim.”

Ignorance of this rule (and method of procedure to
adopt for obtaining increased pension) on the part of a
certain gentleman who interested himself on behalf
of the old man from Ballindesart, was the cause of
the whole delay in obtaining the increased pension.
This gentleman in question sent on all the pensioner’s

_ documents at first to the Local Government Board,

and after having been kept for some time by that body
were returned to the pensioner. 'The documents were
then forwarded to me to a wrong address, but when I
got them I wrote back to pensioner by veturn post
giving him the proper directions as to how to proceed,
etc., according to the rules. The old man then came
to me in a day or two afterwards for a consultation,
and was most profuse in his thanks on his departure
for giving him all the information and instruction in
my power. The report from the Pension Officer on
his claim was received by me for the Sub-Committee
on the 24th February last, and -a meeting of the Sub-
Committee was summoned for the 29th February for
the consideration of this and the other claims received.
This old man’s claim was duly considered on latter

date, and as the result, his pension was increased to
the ruaximum rate. A notice to that effect was du!y
posted.to the pensioner on the following morning.. Did.
this action show sleepiness on part of clerk and Sub-
Committee ?

The Sub-Committee receives the reports on the pen-
sion claims on last Saturday of every ‘month from the
supervisor of the district, and & meeting of Sub-Com-
mittee is then summoned for the Thursday following
for the consideration and decision of such claims.

It may happen that in some months no reports on
claims would be received from the supervisor, and
then, of course, there would be no meeting of the Sub-
Committee, bub the statement that the Sub-Committee
“do nob meet sometimes for three months’ is abso-
lutely untrue, as the minutes of meetings held can
show. The Sub-Committee and clerk surely are not
accountable for the delays on the part of the pension
officers in forwarding their reports on claims to us,
and if “scurrilous’’ paragraphs and ‘‘mustard plasters’
should have any effect in stirring them up, I am sure
we shall be only too well pleased.

I have much pleasure in giving you the names of
this model Sub-Committee, and they' are as follows:—
Very Rev. P. Canon Power, P.P. (Chairman); Messrs.
B. Nowis, J.P.; M. Walshe, J.P.; J. Sheehan, J.P.;
J. O’Donnell, M.C.C.; W. Nugent, D.C.; M. Con-
nolly, D.C.; D. Flynn, D.C.

It is not my province to say anything as regards
the attendance or non-attendance of the foregoing at
the meetings. 1 am only their executive officer and
I consider it very unfair for your informant to make
such unwarranted statements as he has made as re-
gards the clerical work in connection with the Sub-
Committee meetings. I hope you, sir, will give pub-
licity to this contradiction of his statements in your
neﬁt issue and you will much oblige.—Yours faith-
fully,

P. WHELAN.
Clerk to Sub-Committee.

LIBERTY, LICENCE AND LITERATURE.

3 H, Liberty, what crimes are committed in thy
. name ! ” The truth contained in this excla-
m_ation of Madame Roland as she passed the statue of
Liberty on her way to execution has so struck the minds
of men that the saying has become one of the most
celebrated ever uttered. Yet new crimes® are being
committed every day under the same name of liberty.
People raise the catch-cry that they are defending the
rights of liberty, when in reality they are defending
licence of the worst and most pernicious kind. Liberty
and licence are two completely distinet things. Liberty
is noble—something to be striven for when absent, to
be defended and fought for when found. But what is
licence ? or to put the question as Burke puts it—* What
is liberty without wisdom and without virtue 2’ He
answers, * It is the greatest ol all possible evils, for it
is folly, vice and madness without tuition or restraint.”
If a man tells you that he is acting in the name of liberty
when he defends another who steals his neighbour’s
purse, e takes his neighbour’s life, or co: hts the morals
of his sdeighbour’s home, do not believe him. He is
acting in the name of licence, or to repeat the words of
that philosopher whose writings will endure when many
of our present-day scribblers are unheard of, he is
upholding “ the greatest of all evils, for it is folly, vice
and madness without tuition or restraint.”

Liberty as some foolish people conceive it—freedom
without guidance or check—would be the ruin of society.
Ruskin, in his “ Seven Lamps,” remarks indeed that its
attainment is an impossibility : “ How false is the
conception, how frantic the pu it of that treacherous
phantom which men call Liberty—most treacherous
indeed of all phantoms, for the feeblest ray of reason
might surely show us that not only its attainment but
its being was impossible. There is no such thing in the
universe. There can never be. The stars have it not ;
the earth has it not; the sea has it not; and we men
have the semblance of it only for our heaviest punish-
ment.”

Yet that unrestrained liberty which is only a phantom
according to Ruskin—which-is folly, vice. and madness
according to Burke—which every thinking man must
see would be the ruin of society—has its defenders in
Ireland to-day.

The literature crusade brought them toelight. Over
the wide world the fact has been recognised that con-
siderable sections of the modern Press have passed the
bounds of true liberty and have become a serious menace
to society. Patriotic men all the world over have banded
themselves together, with. government authority or by
voluntary association, to safeguard the highest and best
interests of their country against the destruction which
inevitably follows a licentious Press. New York, Paris,
Rome and London are cities which may well be taken to
represent the world, and each of these has had in one
shape or another its literature crusade.®

in word and act, each member binds himself (a) never to
buy or read writings which aie contrary to the principles
of Christian decency ; (b) never to buy any book or

Why was it that we heard nothing of that catch-cry
while similar campaigns were being carried on in other
countries? London is not accused of being an, enemy
to liberty, and New York is the capital of the freest
country in the world. Why then is an Irish city accused
of intolerance for following the example of the two cities
just mentioned ? The accusation shows how  superior ”
is the reasoning of some people, and of people, too, who
have no concern in the business. Many of the Irish
newsagents (all honour to them) were only too glad that
the traffic was stopped.

When the crusade was heing discussed at first some
people proposed that not only immoral literature but
anti-Christian and anarchic literature also should be
attacked. They made their propositions openly, and
their words were borne far and wide in the pages of
the public Press. The same * superior ”’ reasoners at once
suspected secret designs to establish a censorship of the
Press that would ruin all liberty of speech in Ireland
for ever; and then, deliberately confusing issues, they
attacked the entire crusade. As a matter ol fact no
Vigilance Committee adopted the proposals, but the fact
that those who ventured the suggestions were exposed
to bitter personal abuse sho to what a pretty pass
the minds of some of our countrymen have come. They
look on the suggestions as something hitherto unheard
of, something absolutely new and fraught with dangers
for the future. When President Roosevelt proposed the
suppression of an anarchic Press no sane American
considered him a menace to the future of the States ;*and
irreligious literature has been again and again condemned
by that Catltolic Church which preserved learning in
Europe when there was no other power to preserve it,
and which lifted many a modern nation from savagery
to civilisation. The Church has made no secret of her
principles ; she has proclaimed them on the house-tops,
and her manner of acting is very clearly explained by
no less an authority than Leo XIII. He writes® :—
“ The excesses of an unbridled intellect which unfailingly
end in the oppression of the untutored multitude are no

less rightly controlled by the authority of the law than *

are injuries inflicted by violence on the weak. And this
all the more.surely because by far the greater part of the
community is either absolutely unable, or able only with
great diffiiculty, to escape from illusions and deceitful
subtleties, especially such as flatter the passions. . . .
If unbridled licence of speech and of writing be granted
to all, nothing will remain sacred and inviolate ; even
the highest and truest mandates of nature—justly held
to be the common and noblest heritage of the human
race—will not be spared. Thus truth being gradually
obscured by darkness, pernicious and manifold error

- will easily prevail. Thus, too, licence will gain what
liberty loses, for liberty will ever be'more frec and secure
~ in proportion as licence is kept in fuller restraint.” The
_last thought of the illustrious Pontiff recalls the epigram
of Tennyson on those who extend licence so far that it
~ ends in the ruin of liberty :—

* Chaos, Cosmos ! Cosmos, chaos ! once again the sickening
game ; :

Freedom—{ree to slay herself, and dying while they shout
her name.”

-I have made this rather long digression on the pro-
posals which were made to include books other than
immoral in the new crusade, not indeed to advocate
their inclusion but to show that there was no new theory
nor anything unpatriotic, and above all anything irrational,
in those proposals. Some people found fault with them,
however, and then—and this is what concerns me most
here—with quite refreshing logic they concluded that
the whole movement should be checked !

It is sad to have to appeal to Englishmen for a saner
notion of things, yet there is a weak-kneed section at
home with whom the fact that an Englishman has made
some statement carries more weight than the intrinsic
arguments of the case. To Englishmen then let us go
to bear out the simple truth that an attack on licence
is not an attack on liberty. The other day I chanced
on.a book written by Mr. H. O. Arnold-Forster with the
purpose of explaining to English children ** the principles
which should actuate them as patriotic citizens.” *Having
discussed such topics as patriotism, justice, law, taxation
and the like, he devotes several sections to the question
of liberty. He glories in the freedom which Englishmen
enjoy—Ireedom of election, freedom of trade, freedom of
the Press. He teaches the rising generation to appreciate
their liberty, but he is most careful to explain what that
liberty is. * Indeed,” he writes (and the italics are his),
“{o put the whole matter quite shortly, we are free to
do anything which is nol conlrary lo law, and which does
nol lend to injure owr neighbours.” Speaking particuls
of the liberty of the Press he says:— The same rule
applies here as in the case of the sale of gunpowder and
the manufacture of vitriol. We are free to do as we like,
as long as by doing s¢ we do not harm other people.”

The elementary distinction thus brought home to
English children does not seem to have been gragped
by the grown-up critics of the Jiterature crusade. When
they tell us that an attack on admittedly pernicious
literature is an attack on liberty they make it evident

THE LIFE OF CARDINAL NEWMAN.
(Concluded.)

ARDINAL NEWMAN was a great Englishman,
C proud of his nation, but he could enter into
the views of Irish Nationalists. He wrote a remark-
able letter when he was eighty-six years of age in
reply to one in which the Jesuit Father Hopkins had
recorded his experience of the:anti-English feeling in
parts of Ireland in the year 1887 :—'* Your letter is an
appalling one—but not on that account untrustworthy.
There is one consideration, however, which you omib.
The Irish patriots hold they never have yielded them-
selves to the sway of England, and therefore never
have been under her laws, and never have been rebels.
This does not diminish the force of your picture, but
it suggests that there is no help, no remedy. If I
were an Irishman, I should be (in heart) a rebel.
Moreover, to clinch the difficulty, the Irish character
and tastes are very different from the English. My
fingers will not let me write more.’”

Cardinal Newman understood the case of Iveland
thoroughly owing to his experience here half a cen-
tury ago. He had a wonderfully clear insight into the
position of the Irish clergy towards the laity, and he
thus expressed himself in a letter to Mr.-John Wallis,
the Iiditor of the ‘‘ Tablet,”” in 1856:—'‘ It seems to
me, speaking in confidence, that no small portion of
the hierarchy and clergy of Ireland think it a mistake
and a misfortune that they have any of the upper or
middle classes among them—that they do but feel
awkward when a gentleman is converted or shows
himself a good Catholic—and in fact that they think
that then only Ireland will become again the Isle of
Saints, when it has a population of peasants ruled
over by a patriotic priesthood patriarchally. Now, I
really do think this a fact. A gentleman is an evil.

fied with the Will that made him Irish.

days of pride and glory, its tales of shame and sorrow—I
learned first from the stories of my grandmother—God
rest her soul—and in later days from any history or
story of Ireland I could lay hands on. My sub-conscious
comparison of ancient and modern periods inspired me
with pride and hope, not sorrow and despair.  Yet I
never peopled Ireland with saints alone, or heroes only,
or the most learned of the earth. The Trish race was
human and, therefore, had in it members imperfect

several of them really brilliant; all are readable by an
average reader; only one of them struck me as being
that most odious and too common 1 Yy exercise, a
man sitting down to be learned, over fi six pages,
on a subject in which he is not really interested.

On the whole, those who are responsible for the new
publication deserve every congratulation for what they
have given us. Tt is to be hoped' that their courage
will achieve its objects and that in the end they will
train our fellow-countrymen into buying the review.
Tor the moment, they.will, T fear, set out to borrow it.

CURRENT AFFAIRS.

A correspondent writes :—"* In ‘ Current Affairs ’ in
last week’s issue of the LEADER, the paragraph dealing
with the annual appeal of the Gaelic League has one
very important slip to which I would like to direct your
attention. 1t states:—‘Out of about 537 teachers
turned out last year by the Training Colleges of the
National Board, only 33 were qualified to teach Irish.
This is killing Trish at the source.” Now, 83 should
have been 83. The figures for the various Colleges
are as follows :—Marlborough Street, 3; St. Patrick’s,
17; Our Lady of Mercy, 7; De La Salle, 28; St. Mary’s,
Belfast, 21; Mary Immaculate, 7. Of these 83, 38
obtained the Bilingual Certificate.” In some of the
Colleges there were student teachers already certified
for Irish, and so debarred from competing for the £5
prize by going again for the examination. De La Salle
had two such. T might also mention that De La Salle
tained 15 of the 30 prizes.”

There seems to be little or no diminution of dances,
balls, dinner parties, theatre performances—dirty and
otherwise—during Lent. Down in Moate, in the Co.

, Westmeath, the Court House has been the scene of a

series of bruising competitions. Some travelling prize-
fighters are engaged in the gentle art of amusing Moate
audiences during some nights of Lent. It is only fair
to Moate to add that they did not attract very large
audiences. Some not only went to see but took part
in the bruising, and one of the latter was rendered
unconscious.
